title: On Zaremba's Conjecture on Continued Fractions
speaker: Jean Bourgain
abstract: Zaremba's 1971 conjecture predicts that every integer appears as the denominator of a finite continued fraction whose partial quotients are bounded by an absolute constant. We confirm this conjecture for a set of density one.

proof_stages.label: Zaremba denominators and the almost-all theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines the fractions with partial quotients bounded by A, the denominator set D_A, states Zaremba's conjecture that D_A should be all integers for some absolute A, and announces the result that A around 50 gives all denominators outside a very small exceptional set, with consequences for primitive residues modulo primes.

proof_stages.label: Fractal dimension and counting heuristic
proof_stages.summary: Continued-fraction Cantor sets with bounded alphabet are assigned their Hausdorff dimension delta_A, Hensley's count |R_A(N)| about N^{2 delta_A} is recalled, and the theorem is reframed for alphabets whose dimension is sufficiently close to 1.

proof_stages.label: Semigroup orbit reformulation
proof_stages.summary: Good fractions are generated by products of the matrices [[0,1],[1,a]], so the problem becomes to understand which integers occur as a coordinate, equivalently a matrix entry, in the orbit of a finitely generated semigroup.

proof_stages.label: Local-to-global circle-method framework
proof_stages.summary: A prior group-case local-to-global theorem motivates the strategy, and the semigroup problem is converted into a Fourier/circle-method question in which major arcs should give a positive main term and minor arcs must have small L2 contribution.

proof_stages.label: Multilinear ensemble construction
proof_stages.summary: The exponential sum over lower-right entries is restricted to a large ensemble Omega_N built as a product of blocks Lambda_L with controlled norm, nearly maximal cardinality N^{2 delta}, and compatible expanding directions.

proof_stages.label: Minor-arc bilinear estimates
proof_stages.summary: The minor arcs are decomposed by denominator and distance scales, a suitable block is singled out to rewrite the exponential sum as a bilinear form over two large two-dimensional projections, and Cauchy-Schwarz/Vinogradov-type estimates yield a power saving in the minor-arc L2 norm.

proof_stages.label: Major arcs and uniform semigroup counting
proof_stages.summary: To obtain density one rather than merely positive proportion, the major-arc analysis is reduced to a counting lemma for semigroup elements with prescribed Archimedean data and residue class modulo q, giving the expected main term divided by |SL_2(q)| plus an effective error.

proof_stages.label: Thermodynamic transfer-operator proof of equidistribution
proof_stages.summary: Symbolic coding and a renewal equation express the counting function through the resolvent of a Ruelle transfer operator, and scalar nonlattice/Dolgopyat ideas together with expansion for SL_2(q)-valued transfer operators provide the uniform modular control needed for the major-arc lemma.

examples.name: Sparse power denominators
examples.note: The known cases such as denominators of the form 2^j and 3^j illustrate earlier explicit folding-algorithm constructions, contrasting with the dynamical/orbit method of the talk.
examples.engagement_reason: the powers such as 2^j and 3^j are cited as known sparse special cases with constructed numerators, but no construction or computation is carried out on them

examples.name: Zaremba low-discrepancy lattice points
examples.note: The point set (j/d, bj/d mod 1) in the unit square shows why bounded partial quotients are useful for producing near-optimal rectangular discrepancy.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker explicitly builds the lattice points (j/d, bj mod d/d), relates bounded partial quotients to a discrepancy bound, and discusses the rectangle discrepancy/optimality mechanism

passes.reason: Zaremba’s rank-one lattice point sets are highly regular and realize the optimal order log(d)/d discrepancy
The Zaremba lattice point sets achieve the Schmidt-optimal rectangular discrepancy order log d/d, so they are structurally extremal and used constructively.
The rank-one modular lattice point sets are highly regular and, under bounded partial quotients, attain the optimal Schmidt-order discrepancy.
the rank-one lattice point sets from bounded continued fractions attain the optimal discrepancy order log d/d
The rank-one lattice point sets from bounded partial quotients attain the optimal log d/d discrepancy order given by Schmidt's lower bound.
the Zaremba lattice rule is used to attain the optimal log d/d discrepancy order
The Zaremba rank-one lattice point sets are used positively and achieve the optimal log n/n discrepancy order.
These rank-one lattice point sets are presented as attaining the optimal log d/d discrepancy order.

These rank-one lattice point sets are presented as attaining the optimal rectangular discrepancy order log d/d.

examples.name: Linear congruential generator plots
examples.note: The homogeneous generator x -> bx mod d, especially with d prime and b primitive, illustrates the applied need for primitive multipliers with bounded continued fractions and is visualized by good and bad scatter plots.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker runs the linear congruential construction via powers of b mod d, analyzes primitive b and pair correlations, and interprets good versus bad plotted examples

passes.reason: the bad linear-congruential plot is used as an enemy case showing primitive modular generators can still distribute poorly without bounded partial quotients

For prime d and primitive b the multiplicative congruential generator has maximal period, while the bad-partial-quotient plot is used as an enemy case showing primitive LCGs need not look well distributed.
The LCG serial plots are deterministic modular-lattice point sets, with the bad-continued-fraction plot serving as an enemy case for good distribution from primitivity alone.
the LCG serial-correlation plots are regular cyclic/lattice orbits, with the bad large-partial-quotient plot used as an adversarial case showing primitive mod d alone need not distribute well
With a primitive multiplier mod prime the LCG has maximal period, and the bad continued-fraction plot is used to show primitive generators alone can still distribute poorly.

examples.name: The A=2 continued-fraction Cantor picture
examples.note: The bounded-partial-quotient Cantor set for alphabet {1,2} and its numerical projections make concrete the dimension threshold and the heuristic that many denominators should appear.
examples.engagement_reason: the A=2 Cantor set is used concretely through its dimension, projected rational-point pictures, and the reasoning about projections toward denominators

passes.reason: the A=2 continued-fraction Cantor set is the minimal nontrivial bounded-quotient case and sits just above the critical half-dimension threshold

examples.name: Goldbach as a circle-method model
examples.note: Goldbach's problem is used as a standard comparison showing how major arcs yield a singular-series main term while Vinogradov-type estimates control minor arcs.
examples.engagement_reason: Goldbach is used as a concrete circle-method model by defining the representation weight, exponential sum, major-arc approximation, and minor-arc Vinogradov input
